name: Supply Chain Vulnerabilities
what_it_means: LLM applications depend on third-party models, datasets, plugins, and infrastructure components that may contain backdoors, poisoned data, or malicious code compromising the entire system.
animated_example: Like buying a pre-assembled computer from an untrusted vendor — the hardware or OS might already have a backdoor installed before you even turn it on.
quick_checks: Are all third-party models, datasets, and plugins from verified, reputable sources?
Is a Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) maintained for all LLM components?
Are model integrity hashes verified before deployment?
Is the supply chain reviewed for changes on each update or dependency upgrade?

payload_types.description: Downloading a model with embedded backdoors or biased behavior from untrusted repositories
examples: Backdoored Hugging Face model with trigger phrase
Fine-tuned model with hidden harmful instruction following
mitigation: Only use models from verified publishers with signed artifacts
Verify model hashes and provenance before deployment

payload_types.description: Third-party LLM plugins that exfiltrate data or execute unauthorized actions
examples: Plugin that silently logs all user queries
Tool integration with hidden data exfiltration
Open-source agent framework with malicious dependency
mitigation: Run plugins in sandboxed environments with minimal permissions

payload_types.description: Malicious or biased data injected into training pipelines to skew model behavior
examples: Scraped web data containing adversarial examples
RAG knowledge base with injected false facts
mitigation: Apply data validation and anomaly detection on all training inputs
Use curated, audited datasets from authoritative sources

payload_types.description: Attacks targeting the model deployment pipeline rather than the model itself
examples: CI/CD compromise substituting model weights at deploy time
Tampered model registry serving modified artifacts
mitigation: Implement code signing and artifact integrity checks in CI/CD
Use immutable model registries with access control

how_to_test: Verify SHA-256 hashes of all downloaded models against official releases
Audit all third-party plugins for data exfiltration, unusual network calls, or excessive permissions
Review training data sources for injected adversarial content or misinformation
Test model behavior with known trigger phrases if sourced from external repositories
Inspect CI/CD pipeline for unauthorized model substitution points
expected_outcomes: All model artifacts are verified with cryptographic signatures before deployment
Third-party plugins are sandboxed and audited; no unauthorized data flows
Training data lineage is tracked and anomalies are flagged
minimal_example: New LLM plugin installed → automated audit flags undocumented API calls → plugin blocked from production
mitigation: Maintain a Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) for every LLM application component — models, datasets, plugins, frameworks, and infrastructure — and review it on every dependency update

Verify cryptographic integrity (SHA-256 hashes or signed artifacts) of all downloaded models, datasets, and plugins before integration; reject artifacts that cannot be verified against a trusted source
Sandbox all third-party plugins and tools with least-privilege network and file system access; audit source code before deployment and monitor runtime behavior for anomalous data flows
Apply data validation, provenance tracking, and anomaly detection to all training and fine-tuning datasets; prefer curated, authoritative data sources and maintain data lineage records
